In April, Burkina Faso's junta leader publicly told citizens to "forget" democracy, calling it deadly — remarks that drew both criticism and some support among residents who feel security and economic needs outweigh electoral politics. Niger, Mali, and Burkina Faso formally left the regional ECOWAS bloc in January 2025 and formed their own alliance.

A UN report links coups to poverty, inequality, poor governance, and public disappointment with elected governments. Sudan remains in civil war between rival military factions, and instability continues across the Horn of Africa and Central Africa.
events.source_title: World | Deutsche Welle
events.prayer: Civilians across the Sahel and Horn of Africa living under military rule, poverty, and political exclusion

events.title: Armed Conflict in eastern Congo
events.body:
Escalating violence in eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo is making it harder to treat and contain Ebola, according to the UN's aid coordination office. Fighting in the region has disrupted humanitarian operations at a time when health workers need safe access to affected communities.

The combination of armed conflict and a disease outbreak puts civilians in a particularly difficult position — unable to reach medical care and cut off from aid workers trying to reach them. UN officials flagged the situation on a Tuesday briefing, though specific locations and casualty figures were not provided in the initial report.

Eastern Congo has faced years of instability driven by multiple armed groups. When violence flares, it delays vaccinations, contact tracing, and basic health services — all critical for stopping an Ebola outbreak before it spreads further.
events.source_title: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
events.prayer: Civilians in eastern Congo caught between ongoing violence and an active Ebola outbreak

events.title: Displacement in Lebanon
events.body:
The UN reproductive health agency (UNFPA) warned this week that women and girls in Lebanon are bearing a heavy toll from continued violence and displacement, even after a ceasefire was reached. The agency said the situation is putting lives at serious risk.

UNFPA's warning focused on the danger facing women and girls who cannot access the healthcare they need during pregnancy, childbirth, and other critical moments. Displacement has left many without clinics, medical staff, or basic supplies.

The agency called for urgent action, saying that delays in responding to these needs cost lives. Communities already struggling with the effects of conflict are now facing a growing health emergency that is falling hardest on women and girls.
events.source_title: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
events.prayer: Women and girls in Lebanon lacking access to reproductive healthcare amid ongoing displacement and violence

events.title: Armed Conflict in Nabatieh
events.body:
Israeli forces have captured Beaufort Castle, a 900-year-old Crusader fortress near the city of Nabatieh in southern Lebanon, as part of what officials describe as the deepest Israeli military advance into the country in 26 years. Troops have crossed north of the Litani River and continued pushing toward the Zahrani River, with heavy fighting reported across the south.

Lebanon's culture minister says Israeli bombardments have struck near the ancient city of Tyre and directly hit Beaufort Castle. Tyre, a UNESCO World Heritage Site containing major Phoenician and Roman ruins, has seen roughly 200,000 people flee the city and surrounding area. Across Lebanon, the broader conflict has displaced more than one million people.

UNESCO officials note that dozens of protected heritage sites in southern Lebanon carry the highest level of legal protection under international law. Several medieval castles and ancient sanctuaries in the region are now within active conflict zones, and further damage could put governments and individuals at risk of criminal responsibility under international conventions.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Roughly 200,000 people displaced from Tyre and surrounding areas amid ongoing bombardment and advancing forces

events.title: Armed Conflict in Qeshm Island
events.body:
Iran fired missiles and drones at Kuwait and Bahrain in the early hours of Wednesday, targeting areas where US military assets are based. Kuwait's international airport was struck, with flight suspensions, facility damage, and injuries reported. At least one Indian national was killed and others were wounded. US officials said some Iranian missiles fell short or broke apart before reaching their targets, and intercepted projectiles may have caused some of the airport damage. No US personnel or assets were reported harmed.

Shortly before the Iranian strikes, US forces hit a telecommunications tower on Iran's Qeshm Island and shot down Iranian drones targeting civilian ships in the Gulf. Iran said a US strike also damaged an Iranian oil tanker near the Strait of Hormuz, and its navy responded by targeting another vessel.

Iran accused Kuwait and Bahrain of allowing their territory to be used for US operations and warned it would respond to future attacks. The flare-up follows a temporary ceasefire that had been brokered by Pakistan in April, and raises fresh concerns for civilians and travelers across the region.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Civilians in Kuwait, Bahrain, and Iran caught in overnight missile strikes and a widening Gulf conflict

events.title: Civil Unrest in Borno State
events.body:
Teachers in north-eastern Nigeria took to the streets to protest the abduction of dozens of schoolchildren in Borno State last month. The demonstrators called on authorities to do more to protect schools and the children who attend them.

Borno State has long been affected by violence that has at times targeted schools and students. The latest abduction has prompted educators to speak out publicly, reflecting deep concern among communities about the safety of children trying to get an education.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Teachers and schoolchildren in Borno State after a mass abduction from a learning institution

events.title: Armed Conflict in Kuwait International Airport
events.body:
Kuwait's government has described a drone attack on the country's international airport as "heinous Iranian aggression." One person was killed and dozens were injured when drones struck a terminal, causing significant material damage.

Kuwait's defence ministry attributed the attack directly to Iran. The strike caused damage at the airport terminal, though the extent of disruption to flights and airport operations was not detailed in available reports.

The attack marks a serious escalation affecting civilian infrastructure. Travelers, airport staff, and families of those injured or killed are dealing with the immediate aftermath.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Dozens injured and one killed in drone strike on Kuwait's international airport

events.title: Sourton, Devon
events.body:
A military helicopter crashed in the early hours of Wednesday morning near the village of Sourton in Devon, killing three Royal Navy crew members. The crash happened around 4:00 a.m. during a training exercise. The Ministry of Defence announced the deaths, saying its thoughts were with the families and friends of those killed.

An investigation has been launched by the navy's leadership. Local police were at the scene, and officials described the incident as ongoing. Few details have been released, as the families of those who died asked for a period of grace before more information is made public.

Several naval bases are located near the crash site, including Western Europe's largest naval base. Senior government officials confirmed they were following the situation closely and said more information would be shared as soon as possible.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Families of three naval crew members killed in a training helicopter crash in Devon

events.title: Civil Unrest in Bakersfield
events.body:
FBI agents fatally shot a man who had barricaded himself with several hostages inside a Bakersfield, California office building that houses a bank branch and school district office. The incident began after police received a bomb threat at the location and lasted roughly 12 hours before ending in a shooting.

During negotiations, two of the hostages were released. When the standoff ended, all remaining hostages were found unharmed and were evaluated by medical personnel at the scene. Authorities evacuated nearby City Hall and police headquarters and established a wide perimeter around the building during the standoff.

Police said the investigation is ongoing and that a significant law enforcement presence would remain in the area. No motive has been released, and the identity of the man who was killed had not been made public at the time of reporting.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Hostages and residents in Bakersfield shaken after a 12-hour standoff and shooting at a public building

events.title: Civil Unrest in Southampton
events.body:
Hundreds of protesters clashed with riot police in Southampton on Tuesday over the December murder of an 18-year-old finance student. Demonstrators threw stones, chairs, rocks, and flares at officers near the Portswood neighborhood where the student died. Police lines were forced to retreat as the crowd grew violent.

The unrest follows a court sentencing on Monday, in which the killer received a life sentence with a minimum of 21 years. A widely circulated police body-camera video shows officers initially disbelieving the stabbed student's repeated cries that he had been injured while he lay handcuffed on the ground. The victim's killer had accused him of racist abuse, a claim the sentencing judge dismissed entirely. National police leaders have since said they will review anti-racism guidance that some say shaped how officers responded.

The victim's own family has asked the public not to use his death to fuel division or hatred. An unconnected officer has received death threats after being wrongly identified in online posts.
events.source_title: World | Deutsche Welle
events.prayer: Families and communities in Southampton divided by grief, anger, and street violence after a student's death

events.title: Armed Conflict in Calabria
events.body:
Four migrant farm workers were killed when two men blocked the doors of their minivan, poured a flammable liquid inside, and set it on fire at a petrol station near a village in Calabria, in southern Italy. Security camera footage captured the attack and led to the arrest of two Pakistani nationals. A fifth man, from Afghanistan, survived by breaking a window.

The survivor told Italian media the victims were three Afghans and a Pakistani, all working in the region's strawberry fields. He said the attack followed a dispute over a transportation fee the workers refused to pay, and that the workers had also not received wages for their labor, though they had been given food and shelter. Italian media reported 14 similar arson incidents involving vehicles carrying Pakistani workers in the same area in recent months, linked to tensions among migrants over access to farm work and housing.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Migrant farm workers in Calabria's strawberry fields, vulnerable to violence and exploitation

events.title: Coup/Crisis in Evin prison, Tehran
events.body:
A British couple arrested in January 2025 while passing through Iran on a round-the-world motorcycle trip has lost an appeal against their 10-year espionage sentence. The two deny the charges. Their family says they were not allowed to attend their own appeal hearing, and no reason was given for the rejection. The case has been passed to Iran's Supreme Court, though the family does not understand the process or timeline.

Both are currently on hunger strike at Tehran's Evin prison. The man has been refusing food for around 25 days and is reported to be visibly weakening. The woman is on day 16 of her hunger strike. The couple has been blocked from contacting their family in the UK for over a month, and their last consular visit was in December.

The UK Foreign Office called the appeal decision disappointing and their detention "unjustified and appalling." It currently warns British citizens that holding a British passport alone can be grounds for detention in Iran.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: British couple on hunger strike in Evin prison after losing appeal against espionage conviction

events.title: Armed Conflict in Vynohradar, Kyiv
events.body:
A large Russian missile and drone attack struck multiple Ukrainian cities overnight, killing at least six people in Kyiv and at least 16 in Dnipro, where two residential buildings were hit directly. More than 90 people were injured across Kyiv and Dnipro, and 10 more were hurt in Kharkiv, including a child. Energy facilities and civilian infrastructure were also hit in Kharkiv.

In Vynohradar, a quiet residential neighborhood of Kyiv, high-rise apartment blocks lost their windows, cars were reduced to burnt wreckage, and several residents were hospitalized with severe injuries. A record number of people — more than 41,000, including nearly 4,500 children — sheltered overnight in the Kyiv metro.

After the attack, rescuers checked on residents, government psychologists spoke with those in shock, and volunteers distributed food. Neighborhood residents and municipal workers worked side by side to clear rubble from a children's activity center damaged in the strike.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Residents of Kyiv, Dnipro, and Kharkiv grieving losses and recovering from a devastating overnight missile and drone attack

events.title: Armed Conflict in Middle Belt
events.body:
New research from a German social science institute, drawing on over two decades of Nigerian data, finds that religious divisions — not climate change alone — are the key driver of violence between Muslim herders and mostly Christian farming communities. Drought and desertification across the Sahel have pushed Fulani pastoralists beyond their traditional grazing lands, but the study finds that violent clashes are most likely where these Muslim herders encounter majority-Christian farming settlements. Where both communities share the same faith, conflicts are less likely to turn deadly.

The research points to rising tensions since the late 1990s, linked to the introduction of Sharia law in parts of northern Nigeria, resistance in the Middle Belt, and the spread of Boko Haram — all of which have deepened mistrust. A US religious freedom body estimates that around 30,000 armed Fulani militants operate across Nigeria, and says violence linked to these groups has caused more deaths among religious communities in the past year than attacks by insurgent groups or criminal gangs.

Researchers are calling for better land and water management, early warning systems, and community-based mediation in religiously mixed areas to keep environmental pressures from escalating into bloodshed.
events.source_title: World | Deutsche Welle
events.prayer: Christian farming communities and Muslim herders in Nigeria's Middle Belt caught between drought pressures and deepening religious distrust

events.title: Armed Conflict in Tyre
events.body:
Israeli strikes continued across southern Lebanon on Tuesday, even as a partial ceasefire brokered by the United States appeared to hold for Beirut. Under the agreement, Israeli forces agreed not to bomb the Lebanese capital in exchange for the armed group Hezbollah not attacking Israel. Fighting continued in the south, with both sides reporting attacks.

On Monday, air strikes hit buildings next to Jabal Amel hospital in Tyre, killing four people and injuring 127, including 39 hospital staff. The hospital's director said there was no warning before the strikes. Inside, corridors were filled with broken glass and collapsed ceiling panels. Incubators were cracked and damaged. The Israeli military said it struck what it called militant infrastructure nearby and said the hospital itself was not targeted.

A dentist and two of his children were killed in a drone strike on a road between Marjayoun and Nabatieh. Israel also issued a fresh evacuation order for Nabatieh town on Tuesday afternoon. Lebanon's health ministry says 128 healthcare workers have been killed in 159 attacks on medical facilities over the past three months.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Civilians near Jabal Amel hospital in Tyre, medical staff, and families displaced by ongoing strikes in southern Lebanon

events.title: Armed Conflict in Ukraine
events.body:
Children across Ukraine are growing up under the constant threat of air raid sirens, with many attending classes in underground shelters or learning online because it is too dangerous to be in regular school buildings. A UN child rights envoy has highlighted the deep psychological toll this ongoing war is placing on young people, who live with the daily fear that an alert could mean death for themselves or their families.

The strain extends beyond physical danger. Years of disrupted schooling and life under threat are leaving lasting marks on children's mental health and development. Many have known nothing but war as a backdrop to their childhood.

The situation means entire generations of Ukrainian children are growing up without the stability and safety that normal development requires, with no clear end in sight to the conditions driving their daily reality.
events.source_title: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
events.prayer: Children across Ukraine living under constant air raid sirens, sheltering in underground schools, and facing lasting psychological harm

events.title: Displacement in Cox's Bazar
events.body:
Nearly nine years after a mass displacement drove more than a million Rohingya people from Myanmar into Bangladesh, the United Nations refugee agency is warning that international support is dangerously close to running out. Most of the refugees live in camps around Cox's Bazar, one of the largest refugee settlements in the world.

The UN is urging governments and donors not to abandon this population as aid cuts threaten to reduce food, shelter, healthcare, and other basic services the camps depend on. Without continued funding, conditions for families already living in difficult circumstances are expected to worsen significantly.

The Rohingya have no clear path to return home, and the situation inside Myanmar has not improved enough to make return safe or voluntary. Refugees in the camps remain almost entirely dependent on international aid to survive.
events.source_title: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
events.prayer: 1.2 million Rohingya refugees in Cox's Bazar camps facing reduced humanitarian aid

events.title: Civil Unrest in Nairobi
events.body:
Demonstrators in Nairobi carried a symbolic coffin labeled "Ebola" through the streets to protest a proposed US quarantine facility planned for Kenya. The site would be used to house Americans who have been exposed to Ebola in the Democratic Republic of Congo.

The protest reflects public concern among residents about hosting a facility connected to an active disease outbreak in a neighboring country. No injuries or arrests were reported in the source material.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Nairobi residents anxious over a proposed foreign quarantine facility in their city

events.title: Syria
events.body:
A Syrian commission has confirmed the deaths of six children who went missing in 2013 during the rule of Bashar al-Assad. The children belonged to a woman identified only as a Syrian mother, and their fate had been unknown for over a decade.

The confirmation came after years of uncertainty for the family and reflects a broader pattern of disappearances that occurred during the Syrian conflict. Many families across Syria have waited years — sometimes without resolution — for any information about loved ones who vanished during that period.

The disclosure is part of ongoing efforts to document what happened to thousands of people who disappeared during years of conflict in Syria. For this mother, the confirmation brings an end to more than twelve years of not knowing.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Six children who disappeared in Syria in 2013, and their grieving mother finally learning the truth>

events.title: Coup/Crisis in Tunis
events.body:
A Tunisian court has sentenced the leader of the Ennahdha opposition party to life in prison plus 30 years on terrorism-related charges. Eleven other defendants received life sentences along with additional prison terms of up to 96 years, and thirteen more were sentenced to between 10 and 48 years. All defendants were also placed under administrative monitoring for five years.

The case stems from a complaint filed in early 2022 connecting Ennahdha's alleged internal network to the assassinations of two prominent opposition politicians in 2013. Ennahdha has denied the accusations, calling them politically motivated, and Tunisian authorities have rejected claims that the prosecutions are politically driven.

The opposition party leader had already received a separate 20-year sentence in April in a different case. In recent months his health deteriorated sharply, prompting his transfer to hospital and calls from opposition groups for his release.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Dozens of opposition defendants in Tunisia facing life sentences amid disputed terrorism charges

events.title: Naxxar
events.body:
An explosion occurred at a fireworks factory in Naxxar on Monday. Two men who were in nearby fields at the time were taken to hospital with minor injuries. All licensed workers connected to the site were accounted for, and none had been on the premises when the blast happened.

Large plumes of smoke were visible rising into the sky following the sound of the explosions. An inquiry has been opened into the cause of the incident. The country's prime minister said his thoughts were with those affected.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Two men injured in a fireworks factory explosion in Naxxar, Malta

events.title: Civil Unrest in Muscatine
events.body:
Six people were killed in a series of shootings across the city of Muscatine, Iowa, on Monday. Police say the attacks appeared to stem from a domestic dispute, with all victims believed to be family members of the suspect. The shootings took place at multiple locations, including a home, a separate residence, and a local business.

Officers responded to the first report just after noon, finding four people dead at a home. The suspected shooter had already left the scene. He was later found on a riverfront trail near a pedestrian bridge with a self-inflicted gunshot wound and was pronounced dead. Investigators then discovered two more victims at separate locations.

The city's police chief called the event an "act of evil." Muscatine is a Mississippi River community of about 23,500 people. The mayor acknowledged the tragedy publicly, saying the community's hearts were heavy after the loss of innocent lives. The investigation is ongoing.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Six family members killed in domestic shooting in Muscatine, Iowa, and the community left grieving

events.title: Armed Conflict in southern Lebanon
events.body:
Despite both sides accepting a US-brokered partial ceasefire plan, fighting has continued in southern Lebanon. Hezbollah fighters struck Israeli tanks in the towns of Haddatha and Bayada with missiles and shells, while Israeli forces intercepted two projectiles fired from Lebanon overnight. A large explosion also rocked the town of Debbine during what was described as a demolition operation.

The partial ceasefire follows weeks of escalating conflict. Lebanon was drawn into the broader regional war on March 2, when Hezbollah launched rockets into Israel following an Israeli strike. At least 3,433 people have been killed in Lebanon since the war began, according to Lebanon's health ministry, which does not separate combatant from civilian deaths. Israel reports 25 soldiers and four civilians killed on its side.

US officials have been working to keep negotiations with Iran separate from the Lebanon conflict, but Iranian officials warned that fighting in Lebanon could jeopardize broader talks. Residents of southern Lebanon and Beirut's suburbs remain caught between ongoing military operations and an uncertain ceasefire.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Over 3,400 people killed in Lebanon since the war began, with fighting continuing despite ceasefire agreement

events.title: Armed Conflict in Dnipro
events.body:
At least 18 people were killed across Ukraine, including two children, in a large overnight missile and drone attack. In the city of Dnipro, 12 people died when an apartment block was struck, with rescuers pulling an eight-year-old boy and a woman from the rubble. Six more people were killed in the capital, Kyiv.

Ukraine's president said Russia launched over 650 drones and 73 missiles of various types in the attack. More than 100 people were injured nationwide. Residential buildings, energy facilities, and civilian infrastructure were hit in multiple regions, including Kharkiv and Zaporizhzhia. Blackouts were reported in Kyiv, and residents sheltered in the city's metro system through the early morning hours.

Russia said the strikes were in response to earlier Ukrainian attacks and warned that similar strikes would continue. Ukraine's president called urgently for additional air defense missiles, saying supplies are critically short.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Civilians in Dnipro and Kyiv, including children, killed and injured in overnight missile and drone strikes

events.title: Armed Conflict in Persian Gulf region
events.body:
Since late February, Iran has fired more than 4,000 missiles and drones at targets across Gulf Cooperation Council countries, with most intercepted by air defenses. Kuwait recently reported its defenses were active against a fresh barrage. The strikes have targeted US military bases and civilian energy infrastructure across the region.

A ceasefire was announced on April 8, and the frequency of strikes has since dropped. But Gulf economies and infrastructure remain under strain. Leaders from Gulf states met in Saudi Arabia in late April — their first such gathering since the conflict began — to coordinate a response and press for Iran's missile and drone capabilities to be reduced as a condition of any lasting agreement.

Analysts note that even if open hostilities end, rebuilding trust will be far harder than simply stopping the fighting. Countries like the UAE, Iraq, and Turkey, which previously helped Iran navigate international sanctions through trade and finance channels, have been alienated by the conflict. The longer-term fallout could reshape trade routes, energy corridors, and regional infrastructure for years.
events.source_title: World | Deutsche Welle
events.prayer: Civilians and communities across Gulf states caught between ongoing missile strikes and deepening regional instability

events.title: Armed Conflict in southern Beirut
events.body:
Fighting between Israeli forces and Hezbollah is continuing in southern Lebanon despite a partial ceasefire agreement. Israeli forces have raised their flag over a fort in southern Lebanon, and strikes have hit the southern suburbs of Beirut, which Israel says is a Hezbollah stronghold. Residents have begun evacuating the area again, and a planned visit by a German government minister was cut short due to the deteriorating security situation.

Lebanese authorities say more than 3,400 people have been killed since early March. Israel says its goal is to weaken and disarm Hezbollah, whose attacks on northern Israel it says continue. Analysts warn that the offensive is weakening Lebanese institutions and narrowing the space for a negotiated settlement.

The Lebanese government, which has limited influence over Hezbollah, has called for the ceasefire to hold and for displaced people to be allowed to return home. Iran has said Israel's military actions are delaying the possibility of a broader ceasefire deal with the United States.
events.source_title: World | Deutsche Welle
events.prayer: Residents fleeing southern Beirut suburbs amid ongoing strikes and a collapsing ceasefire

events.title: Civil Unrest in Mossel Bay
events.body:
Two men from Mozambique were killed in Mossel Bay, South Africa, following violent protests against illegal migration. South African police have not confirmed a motive, but the Mozambican government attributed the deaths to xenophobic attacks and stated that five of its nationals were killed and roughly 800 were affected by Friday's violence.

The unrest included the burning of more than 50 shacks in a settlement in KwaNonqaba, where residents described fleeing with only what they could carry. Five people were arrested in connection with the arson. The Mozambican government said 300 of its citizens had already returned home by Saturday, with 500 more expected to follow.

Several neighboring countries have advised their citizens to remain vigilant, and Ghana has already evacuated hundreds of its nationals. A protest group has set an informal deadline of June 30 for undocumented migrants to leave, and demonstrations are expected to continue ahead of upcoming local elections.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Mozambican families displaced and killed amid anti-foreigner violence in Mossel Bay and the Western Cape

events.title: Armed Conflict in Qeshm Island
events.body:
Iran entered peace negotiations with the United States after a three-month war that struck water infrastructure across the country. A freshwater desalination plant on Qeshm Island in the Strait of Hormuz was bombed, cutting water supplies to 30 villages. Pipelines and other civilian infrastructure were also hit during the conflict.

The war compounded a crisis already years in the making. Iran had endured five consecutive years of drought by November 2025, leaving 19 major dams completely dry and Tehran's main reservoir at only 8 percent capacity. Water shortages sparked protests in December 2025 and January 2026. The government had warned that Tehran might need to be evacuated if rains did not come.

Reconstruction after the war is expected to make things worse, as resources are diverted away from water management. Researchers also estimated the conflict released nearly 5.6 million tonnes of greenhouse gases between late February and mid-March, adding further environmental pressure to an already strained system.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Millions of Iranians facing severe water shortages worsened by strikes on civilian infrastructure

events.title: Displacement in Vakaga
events.body:
Tens of thousands of people have fled fighting in Sudan's Darfur region into the remote Vakaga province of the Central African Republic. Many arrived after walking for days through the bush, some while pregnant. The Central African Republic already has one of the world's highest maternal mortality rates, and the sudden influx has pushed the few working health facilities to their limits.

In and around the border town of Birao, a small number of clinics supported by the United Nations Population Fund offer prenatal care, emergency delivery services, and basic obstetric support for both refugees and local residents. These services depend heavily on international funding, including US contributions that help pay for midwives, medicines, and equipment. Recent cuts to US foreign assistance have forced aid organizations to reduce overnight staffing and scale back outreach, pushing more women to give birth at home without skilled help.

Complications such as severe bleeding, obstructed labor, and dangerous blood pressure conditions are common, and without quick medical care they can be fatal. Poor roads, insecurity, and a lack of ambulances make reaching any clinic difficult for local women as well.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Sudanese refugee women in Vakaga facing life-threatening childbirth conditions amid funding cuts

events.title: Armed Conflict in Beirut
events.body:
The United States announced that Israel and Hezbollah have agreed to halt attacks following indirect talks. Under the proposed arrangement, Israel would stop strikes on Beirut and its southern suburbs while Hezbollah would stop firing on Israel. Lebanon's embassy in Washington confirmed that Hezbollah accepted the proposal, and a Hezbollah official publicly supported a full ceasefire across all Lebanese territory.

However, the agreement remains uncertain. Israel's government stated it reserves the right to strike Beirut if Hezbollah attacks continue, and Israeli forces have continued shelling in southern Lebanon, hitting areas near Nabatieh and several villages. Previous ceasefire attempts, including a truce announced in late April, collapsed without stopping the fighting.

Since early March, more than 3,400 people have been killed and over 10,000 wounded in Lebanon, according to Lebanese health officials. More than one million people have been displaced. As of Tuesday morning, no new Israeli strikes on Beirut had been reported.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Over one million Lebanese displaced, more than 3,400 killed, as a fragile truce takes shape

events.title: Armed Conflict in Nabatieh
events.body:
At least five people were killed in Israeli drone and air strikes across southern Lebanon, Lebanese state media reported. Two workers were killed at a plant nursery in Jebchit, two more were struck on a street and in a car in the Ansar area, and a driver was killed separately in Nabatieh — all on the same day a ceasefire agreement was announced by U.S. President Donald Trump.

Trump said he had spoken separately with Israeli and Hezbollah leaders and that both sides had agreed to stop fighting. Under the reported terms, Hezbollah would halt cross-border fire into Israel while Israeli forces would stop striking the southern suburbs of Beirut. However, neither Israel nor Hezbollah publicly accepted the deal, and strikes continued on both sides within hours of the announcement.

Israeli forces have pushed deeper into southern Lebanon in recent weeks, including seizing a historic hilltop castle. A ceasefire agreed in April has not held. Negotiations between Israeli and Lebanese delegations were scheduled to continue in the days ahead, but conditions on the ground remain unstable for civilians.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Civilians in southern Lebanon caught in ongoing strikes amid a fragile and contested ceasefire

events.title: Armed Conflict in Kyiv
events.body:
At least five people were killed and dozens injured after Russia launched a major overnight strike on cities across Ukraine. Four people died and 16 were wounded in Dnipro. In Kyiv, one person was killed and at least 29 were injured as ballistic missiles and drones struck the capital in the early morning hours. Two high-rise apartment buildings were hit, with fears that people remain trapped under rubble. Thousands sought shelter as fires and blackouts spread across the city.

An industrial facility in Zaporizhzhia was also attacked. Air raid warnings were active across most of the country. Russia had warned last week of "systematic strikes" following a drone attack on a dormitory in Russian-held Luhansk that killed 21 people. Ukraine's president had warned residents the previous evening that a massive strike was possible and urged close attention to alerts.

The full extent of damage across Ukraine was not yet clear at the time of reporting.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Residents of Kyiv and Dnipro trapped, wounded, and sheltering after overnight missile and drone strikes

events.title: Armed Conflict in Ukraine
events.body:
A senior United Nations official addressed the Security Council, warning that the war in Ukraine continues to escalate dangerously. The official stressed the need for restraint and dialogue as attacks in the conflict have increased.

The UN has consistently called on all parties to step back from further escalation, urging a path toward negotiation rather than continued fighting. The Security Council session reflected growing international concern over the direction of the conflict.

No specific locations or casualty figures were mentioned in the briefing, but the message from UN leadership was clear: the current trajectory poses serious risks, and the international community is watching closely as civilians continue to bear the burden of ongoing hostilities.
events.source_title: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
events.prayer: Ukrainian civilians amid rising attacks and calls for restraint and dialogue

events.title: Coup/Crisis in Copenhagen
events.body:
Denmark's Social Democratic leader has secured a third consecutive term as prime minister, ending more than two months of political deadlock that followed a fractured March general election. The agreement came after more than 60 days of negotiations involving 12 parties. Her party's representation dropped to its lowest level since 1903, leaving her to lead a minority government with limited parliamentary backing.

The new government takes office in the middle of a serious dispute with the United States over the future of Greenland, a self-governed Danish territory. The US president has made repeated threats to annex Greenland, and Denmark's leader has flatly rejected any transfer of sovereignty, warning that a US takeover would undermine NATO.

The government also faces pressure to strengthen Denmark's military amid Russia's ongoing war in Ukraine. Denmark has already increased defence spending above three percent of its economy and expanded military conscription to include women. Managing all of these overlapping pressures will define the new government's early months in office.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Danish and Greenlandic people amid deepening tension over Greenland's sovereignty and future

events.title: Civil Unrest in Valparaiso
events.body:
Labour unions and student groups took to the streets in Valparaiso as the country's president delivered his first State of the Nation address. Demonstrators clashed with police during the speech, with protesters accusing the government of cutting public healthcare, education, and other social programs.

The unrest reflects deep public anger over the direction of government spending. Civilians who depend on those programs are concerned about what the cuts will mean for their daily lives.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Protesters and police caught in clashes over cuts to healthcare, education, and social programs in Chile

events.title: Civil Unrest in West Jerusalem
events.body:
Tens of thousands of ultra-Orthodox Jewish protesters blocked major roads in West Jerusalem, demonstrating against laws requiring most Jewish Israelis to serve in the military. Police responded with water cannon, stun grenades, and mounted officers to disperse the crowds. Officers were seen pulling protesters from beneath a bus after demonstrators lay down under vehicles to prevent traffic from moving.

The confrontations reflect long-running tensions over whether ultra-Orthodox men should be exempt from mandatory military service, an exemption that has been a source of ongoing legal and political dispute in Israel. The scale of turnout and the intensity of the police response point to deep divisions over the issue within Israeli society.

Residents of affected neighborhoods faced significant road disruptions as the demonstrations shut down major routes through the city.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Tens of thousands of ultra-Orthodox protesters in Jerusalem caught between conscience and compulsory military service

events.title: Civil Unrest in South Africa
events.body:
Anti-migrant protests have been building in South Africa in recent weeks, with demonstrators calling on the government to crack down on undocumented migrants and demanding that they leave the country by June 30. Protesters have accused migrants of straining public services and being linked to crime. South Africa's government has condemned what it called criminal acts against foreign nationals, though authorities have not confirmed specific attacks on foreigners.

Nigeria's police force issued a public warning urging citizens not to target South African nationals, businesses, or diplomatic facilities inside Nigeria in retaliation. Officials said any such acts would be treated as crimes, and that extra security has been placed around foreign missions and key sites. Ghana recently evacuated hundreds of its nationals from South Africa, citing safety concerns.

Past episodes of xenophobic violence in South Africa have led to retaliatory attacks on South African-owned businesses in Nigeria, and authorities on both sides appear to be working to prevent a repeat of that cycle.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Nigerian and other African migrants in South Africa facing anti-migrant protests and uncertain safety

events.title: Coup/Crisis in Ouagadougou
events.body:
Burkina Faso's military leader, who came to power in a 2022 coup, told citizens in April to "forget" about democracy, saying it "kills." Speaking on state television, he argued that fighting jihadist violence and rebuilding the economy must come first. Some residents in the capital agreed, while others were troubled by the statement.

The remarks come as military takeovers have reshaped much of Africa. Between 2020 and 2025, soldiers seized power in Mali, Burkina Faso, Niger, Guinea, Gabon, and Guinea-Bissau. In January 2025, Niger, Mali, and Burkina Faso formally left the regional bloc ECOWAS and formed their own alliance. Sudan descended into full civil war between rival military factions. A UN development report points to poverty, inequality, weak governance, and public frustration with elected governments as conditions that make coups more likely.

Across the continent, press freedom is shrinking and opposition voices face persecution in several countries. Civil society organizations note that many governing structures were shaped by colonial-era designs that do not reflect local cultures or meet basic needs for food, water, and education.
events.source_title: World | Deutsche Welle
events.prayer: Civilians across the Sahel and Central Africa living under military rule amid poverty and unrest

events.title: Armed Conflict in Beirut
events.body:
Lebanon announced that Hezbollah accepted a US proposal for both sides to stop attacking each other — Hezbollah halting attacks on Israel, and Israel stopping strikes on Beirut's southern suburbs. The US president said he had spoken to both Israeli leadership and Hezbollah representatives, and that "all shooting will stop." Israel's prime minister confirmed the arrangement but warned strikes on Beirut would continue if attacks on Israeli cities did not stop.

Despite the announced halt, fighting continued. Hezbollah said it launched drone and artillery attacks near two villages in northern Israel. The Israeli military said it intercepted two projectiles fired from Lebanon. Israeli strikes also hit several areas of southern Lebanon, with reports of a large explosion in the town of Debbine.

Iran has warned that Israeli military action in Lebanon threatens a separate US-Iran truce that took effect in early April. Iranian state-linked media raised the possibility of suspending US-Iran talks and opening additional fronts, including near the Red Sea. Residents across the region remain caught between overlapping ceasefires that have so far failed to end the fighting.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Civilians in Beirut and northern Israel caught between renewed strikes and a fragile ceasefire agreement

events.title: Armed Conflict in Dahiye, southern Beirut
events.body:
Iranian officials warned Monday that Israel's escalating attacks in Lebanon and ongoing hostilities in Gaza are jeopardizing ceasefire negotiations with the United States. Iran's foreign minister stated publicly that any ceasefire with the US applies to all fronts, including Lebanon, and that violations would carry consequences. Iranian negotiators also suspended indirect text exchanges with US counterparts, saying talks would not resume until Israeli military operations halt in both Gaza and Lebanon.

On the ground, Israeli forces issued displacement orders for residents of the Dahiye suburbs in southern Beirut and carried out strikes there. Israeli ground forces had the previous day reached their deepest position inside Lebanon in 26 years. The US president said he intervened, posting that Israeli troops would not advance on Beirut and that an agreement to stop shooting had been reached with Hezbollah.

Iran's Revolutionary Guard separately threatened to open new fronts and keep the Strait of Hormuz closed if Israeli military action continues, calling further advances in Lebanon and Gaza a threshold for direct war.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Residents of southern Beirut suburbs and civilians in Lebanon and Gaza amid deepening military operations

events.title: Displacement in Lebanon
events.body:
A community-run grocery store in Lebanon is helping hundreds of families access affordable food, medicine, and basic supplies. The not-for-profit, called "Man wa Salwa," was set up to support people struggling through a combination of displacement and a broader economic crisis.

The store operates as families continue to face hardship linked to ongoing conflict and economic pressures. Many households have been uprooted from their homes and are relying on community efforts to meet daily needs.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Hundreds of Lebanese families facing displacement and rising costs amid ongoing conflict

events.title: Armed Conflict in Tyre
events.body:
An Israeli strike near Jabal Amel Hospital in Tyre, southern Lebanon, has damaged the facility, destroying wards and medical equipment. The attack was described as deadly.

The strike is part of a broader escalation of Israeli attacks across southern Lebanon and the capital Beirut. Hospitals and medical infrastructure being damaged raises serious concerns for civilians who depend on these facilities for care in an already dangerous environment.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Patients and medical staff at Jabal Amel Hospital in Tyre after a strike destroyed wards and equipment

events.title: Civil Unrest in Nanyuki
events.body:
Two people were shot and killed in the Kenyan town of Nanyuki during protests against a US plan to build an Ebola isolation centre at a nearby military base. One man was shot in an area close to the Laikipia Airbase during a demonstration and died after being brought to a local hospital. The other was already dead when soldiers brought him in. Both bodies showed gunshot wounds. A police spokesman said he was not aware of any deaths, and officials have not publicly commented.

The protests began after the US announced plans for a 50-bed facility staffed by American medical workers, intended to treat US citizens affected by the ongoing Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of Congo. Kenya has recorded no Ebola cases. A High Court suspended the plan after a rights group raised public health concerns, and on Tuesday extended that suspension, ordering the government to release details of the agreement. Despite the court order, military aircraft have continued flying in and out of the airbase.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Two men shot dead in Nanyuki amid protests over a proposed Ebola facility near a military base

events.title: Civil Unrest in Nairobi
events.body:
Thousands of people marched through central Nairobi on Monday to demand the government declare a national emergency over rising killings of women and disappearances of children. The march, organized by the End Femicide movement along with women's rights and child protection groups, was one of the largest demonstrations against gender-based violence the capital has seen in months. Organizers pointed to the killing of a gospel singer who was set on fire by three men as she walked home on May 16; she died two days later from burns covering more than 85 percent of her body.

Protest groups have given the government 40 days to act or face nationwide demonstrations. Lawyers' organizations report handling roughly 70 gender-based violence cases every week, and official data shows more than 10,500 child protection cases between January 2025 and March 2026, with nearly 2,328 children currently unaccounted for.

Authorities announced the formation of a dedicated investigative unit combining homicide investigators, forensic experts, and criminal intelligence analysts. Protesters say proper investigations and stronger protections for families are still urgently needed.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Women and children in Kenya amid a surge in gender-based violence and thousands of unaccounted-for children

events.title: Civil Unrest in central Kenya
events.body:
Hundreds of people gathered in central Kenya to protest a plan that would have American citizens exposed to Ebola quarantined in the country rather than returned home. Protesters opposed the US-funded facility being placed in their communities.

Kenya's High Court has temporarily suspended the plan in response to the public outcry, meaning the quarantine centre cannot move forward for now while legal proceedings continue.

Residents remain concerned about what hosting such a facility could mean for local health and safety.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Kenyan communities facing concerns over a foreign quarantine facility planned in their region

events.title: Managua
events.body:
A prominent indigenous leader in Nicaragua has died after nearly three years in government detention. He was 73 years old and had founded Yatama, the country's main indigenous rights movement. Officials said he died from physical and neurological deterioration connected to a Covid-19 infection, but rights groups say harsh conditions in custody contributed to his death.

The government waited 15 hours to confirm the death and, according to opposition media, has refused to release his body to his family. He had been held since September 2023, though the government did not acknowledge his detention until more than a year later. His condition became public only days before his death, when officials admitted he had been hospitalized in Managua since early March with brain swelling, respiratory infection, and kidney failure.

Human rights organizations including Amnesty International and the Inter-American Legal Assistance Center have condemned his treatment and called for accountability. The US State Department described his imprisonment as unjust. His death adds to a growing number of reported dissident deaths in Nicaraguan government custody.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Family of an indigenous leader who died in Nicaraguan government custody after years of detention

events.title: Civil Unrest in Paris
events.body:
Violent clashes broke out across France after a football club won a major European championship final. Nearly 800 people were arrested and 219 were injured, including 57 police officers. One person died in an accident on the city's ring road, which rioters attempted to block overnight, and a teenager was left in critical condition after a separate brawl.

Footage from the city center showed flares being set off, electric bikes burned in the streets, and at least one shopfront smashed. Police fired tear gas to disperse crowds. Authorities said many of those causing trouble were not connected to the football celebrations at all.

More than 6,000 officers were deployed for Sunday's victory parade, and the official celebrations near the Eiffel Tower appeared largely peaceful. However, transport services had already been disrupted overnight, and authorities warned their response to any further violence would be firm. Similar unrest followed the same team's championship win the previous year.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Hundreds injured and one person dead amid post-match riots across Paris

events.title: Armed Conflict in Dahieh
events.body:
Israel's prime minister has ordered strikes on Dahieh, a southern suburb of Beirut and Hezbollah stronghold, citing attacks on Israeli civilians and violations of a ceasefire reached in April. A Lebanese government official told the BBC that Lebanon is relying on US efforts to pressure Israel to halt further civilian casualties. The US Secretary of State spoke with both Israeli and Lebanese leaders over the weekend, proposing that Lebanese officials press Hezbollah to stop attacks on Israel, in exchange for Israel stepping back from escalation in Beirut.

Israeli troops also crossed the Litani River in southern Lebanon to take control of Beaufort Castle, a historically significant hilltop site. Lebanon's defense minister said the campaign was "not over." Lebanon's prime minister accused Israel of collective punishment.

Lebanon was drawn into conflict on March 2 when Hezbollah launched rockets into Israel. Since then, at least 3,371 people have been killed in Lebanon, according to that country's health ministry, with figures not separating combatants from civilians.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Civilians in Beirut and southern Lebanon amid ongoing airstrikes and ground fighting

events.title: Armed Conflict in Middle East
events.body:
Satellite images and video analysed by BBC Verify show that Iranian strikes have damaged at least 20 US military sites across eight countries in the Middle East since late February — more than American officials have publicly acknowledged. The affected facilities are located in Saudi Arabia, the UAE, Qatar, Kuwait, Iraq, Jordan, Bahrain, and Oman.

Among the hardware damaged are three advanced anti-ballistic missile defense systems and multiple refuelling and surveillance aircraft, including an aircraft analysts estimated could cost up to $700 million to replace. Fuel storage bunkers, aircraft hangars, and troop accommodations have also been destroyed or damaged at several sites. The Pentagon has estimated the total cost of the broader conflict at $29 billion, with much expected to go toward replacing destroyed equipment.

The US has asked a major satellite imagery provider to restrict new images of Iran and much of the Middle East. BBC Verify used images from other international providers to document the damage. Some analysts believe the number of bases hit may be as high as 28.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Military personnel and civilians near US bases across the Middle East amid ongoing strikes and counter-strikes

events.title: Civil Unrest in Chimoio
events.body:
Two opposition party members were shot and killed in Mozambique in May 2025. One was killed outside his home in Chimoio, Manica province, on May 9. Another was shot in his home in Gaza province on May 15. Witnesses described the attackers as "ruthless and professional." The opposition party ANAMOLA says 56 of its members have been killed since the party was founded in August 2025, with 436 additional incidents of violence recorded, including assaults and arson attacks.

The killings follow a disputed October 2024 election and nationwide protests. ANAMOLA's leader has accused the government and security forces of being "at least partly responsible." The government denies involvement, calling the incidents isolated cases and saying it does not support extrajudicial killings.

Civil society groups are calling for independent investigations. Human rights observers describe a long pattern of political violence in Mozambique, with allegations of organized groups within the security forces targeting critics and opposition figures going back at least a decade. Represented opposition parties in parliament have also raised alarms about the situation.
events.source_title: World | Deutsche Welle
events.prayer: Opposition members and civil society voices in Mozambique facing politically motivated killings and threats

events.title: Armed Conflict in Kharkiv
events.body:
Residents of Kharkiv, Ukraine's second-largest city, face daily missile and drone attacks that strike homes, factories, and roads. Air raid sirens interrupt sleep throughout the night, and people rush into metro stations for shelter when bombardments begin.

Children in the city continue their schooling underground to stay safe from the strikes. Despite the ongoing danger, residents and city officials are working to rebuild and plan for a future after the conflict ends.

The constant bombardment has made ordinary life deeply unpredictable, with no clear end to the threat for the hundreds of thousands of people still living in the city.
events.source_title: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
events.prayer: Kharkiv residents enduring daily missile strikes, disrupted sleep, and children forced to study underground

events.title: Armed Conflict in Gaza
events.body:
Families living on or near the "Yellow Line" — a boundary controlled by the Israeli military inside Gaza — have told United Nations officials they live in constant fear of being killed or injured. The area has become a source of ongoing dread for residents who remain in proximity to military control zones.

The UN gathered these accounts as part of its continued efforts to document civilian conditions across Gaza. Families described the psychological and physical toll of living so close to active military boundaries, where the risk of harm is ever-present.

Civilians in these areas face daily uncertainty about their safety, with little ability to move away from the danger.
events.source_title: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
events.prayer: Families in Gaza living along the Yellow Line, caught between fear and daily survival

events.title: Armed Conflict in Iran
events.body:
The United States military says it has struck Iranian military sites, and Iran's Revolutionary Guard says it responded by targeting a US base. The strikes are the latest exchange in a conflict now in its 94th day. Kuwait also intercepted missiles during this period, according to the report's headline.

Negotiations to end the war are ongoing, but no deal has been reached. Iran's foreign minister called deal talk "speculation," while the US president said Iran "really wants to make a deal." The two sides appear to be far apart on terms, with the US seeking changes to a proposed memorandum of understanding.

Separately, Israel has deepened its offensive into southern Lebanon during this same period, widening the broader regional picture for people living across the Middle East.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Civilians caught in ongoing US-Iran military exchanges as peace talks stall

events.title: Armed Conflict in Dahiyeh, Beirut
events.body:
Large numbers of people began leaving Dahiyeh, the southern suburb of Beirut, after Israeli officials announced orders for military strikes on the area early Monday morning. Residents packed belongings and jammed roads leading out of the neighborhood. Government-run shelters are already full, leaving many people with no clear destination and forcing some to wait in their cars.

Israeli ground forces have also pushed deeper into southern Lebanon, reaching their furthest point in more than 25 years and taking control of a medieval castle just north of the Litani River. Israeli officials stated an intent to establish a military-controlled zone in that river area.

The United States has put forward a proposal aimed at halting the fighting, which would involve a stop to attacks from both sides in and around Beirut. Separate diplomatic conversations have taken place between Washington and both the Lebanese president and Israeli prime minister, though no agreement has been announced.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Residents of southern Beirut fleeing with nowhere to go as strikes are ordered on their neighborhoods

events.title: Armed Conflict in Sirik Island
events.body:
Iran's Revolutionary Guard (IRGC) launched a strike on a military base it said was used for a US attack on a communications tower on Sirik Island in Iran's Hormozgan Province. The IRGC stated that the targeted base was destroyed, though it did not identify its location. US military officials said no American troops were harmed in the Iranian strike.

Kuwait reported that its air defences intercepted Iranian missiles and drones as sirens sounded across the country. Kuwait's Foreign Ministry condemned the attacks on its territory, warning that continued strikes undermine efforts to reduce tensions and threaten regional stability.

The exchange is the latest in several rounds of fire between US and Iranian forces in recent days. Both sides are in indirect talks about extending a fragile ceasefire that took effect in April, though negotiations have been complicated by disputes over a proposed agreement, mixed signals from both governments, and ongoing Israeli military operations in Lebanon.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Civilians in Kuwait and Iran caught in escalating missile and drone exchanges

events.title: Armed Conflict in Wadi al-Hujeir
events.body:
A well-known resort and restaurant in Wadi al-Hujeir, southern Lebanon, was destroyed in an Israeli airstrike. The site, called Arch Almoulouk, was owned by a prominent Lebanese chef and was widely considered one of the most beloved landmarks in the south of the country.

During the ongoing conflict, the restaurant had been serving free meals to families who had been displaced by the fighting. Images shared after the strike showed the building reduced to rubble.

The loss removes what had become an informal relief point for civilians already uprooted by the war.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Displaced families in southern Lebanon who lost a source of free meals and shelter

events.title: Armed Conflict in Kuwait
events.body:
Kuwait activated its air defences after an attack its foreign ministry described as a "heinous Iranian attack." The incident marked a direct strike on Kuwaiti territory amid a broader exchange of military strikes between Iran and the United States.

Iran stated it targeted a U.S. base in Kuwait in retaliation for what it described as that base being used to launch recent attacks on Iranian territory. The United States and Iran have been trading strikes, according to the source, though the specific scale and targets of each exchange are not detailed.

Kuwait's public condemnation signals the conflict is now directly affecting a third country, raising concerns for civilians and workers living near military installations in the region.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Civilians in Kuwait caught in escalating strikes between Iran and the United States

events.title: Armed Conflict in southern Ukraine
events.body:
Ukraine has confirmed at least 14 drone strikes on Russian supply vehicles in occupied southern Ukraine over the past week. The targeted vehicles were carrying food, fuel, and ammunition along key roads connecting Russia to Crimea and other occupied territories. Analysts have documented the destruction of roughly 150 vehicles more than 12 miles from the front line, though this is believed to represent only about half of all incidents.

Ukraine is using a new AI-enabled drone system trained on thousands of hours of footage of Russian military vehicles. The drones can operate over 100 miles from launch points and connect through satellite networks that are harder for Russian forces to jam. In response, Russia has reportedly shortened its supply convoys to reduce potential losses.

Experts say cutting off ammunition, fuel, and food far behind the front line creates serious difficulties for Russian units fighting at the front and launching longer-range strikes. Analysis from the Institute for the Study of War suggests Ukraine has recently begun regaining slightly more ground than it is losing for the first time since 2023.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Ukrainian and Russian soldiers along supply routes in southern Ukraine amid intensifying drone strikes

events.title: Civil Unrest in Paris
events.body:
More than 400 people were arrested across France after celebrations following a Paris Saint-Germain Champions League victory turned violent. Thousands of officers were deployed in Paris, where crowds set off fireworks and flares, burned electric bikes, and smashed at least one shopfront. Police used tear gas to disperse crowds in the city center, and seven officers were reported injured.

Unrest began earlier in the day when clashes broke out between police and supporters gathered to watch the match on large screens at the team's stadium. By the early hours of Sunday, 280 of the 416 total arrests had been made in Paris alone. Six vehicles, two businesses, and a bus shelter were damaged.

This marks the second consecutive year that PSG's European victory has been followed by serious disorder. Last year's celebrations left two people dead, including a teenager. A victory parade was planned for Sunday afternoon, with players set to tour central Paris and attend a reception hosted by the French president.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Residents in Paris caught in post-match violence, injured officers, and business owners with damaged properties

events.title: Denali National Park and Preserve
events.body:
Three climbers from Latvia died after falling near Denali Pass on Denali — North America's tallest peak — in Alaska. A group of seven was ascending the popular West Buttress route when four of them fell at an altitude of over 5,500 meters on Wednesday. Three of those four died. The remaining three members of the group stayed to help before retreating to a lower camp.

A fourth climber who survived the fall was rescued from a mountain basin at roughly 5,240 meters late Thursday. Because the terrain prevented a helicopter from landing, rescue personnel used a long line to lift the survivor out. The person was then transferred to an air ambulance and taken to a hospital in critical condition.

The Latvian Mountaineering Association called the three who died "talented and experienced" climbers and described the loss as devastating to the broader Latvian mountaineering community.
events.source_title: World | Deutsche Welle
events.prayer: Latvian mountaineering community and families grieving three climbers lost on Denali

events.title: Displacement in Laghman Province
events.body:
A truck carrying recently returned Afghan refugees overturned in Laghman Province in eastern Afghanistan, killing at least 22 people and injuring 36 others. Officials say the driver lost control of the vehicle, and an investigation has been launched.

The victims were refugees who had recently returned to Afghanistan. The crash adds to the hardships already faced by displaced Afghans making their way home under difficult circumstances.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Families of 22 Afghan refugees killed in a truck accident in Laghman Province, and 36 others injured

events.title: Armed Conflict in Galati
events.body:
A drone struck a building in Galati, a major Romanian port city on the Danube River near the Ukrainian border. Romania's president blamed Russia for the attack, and the European Union quickly condemned Moscow. NATO stated it was prepared to defend allied territory in response.

Russia's president denied responsibility but suggested the war was approaching its end. Ukraine's president warned that Russia is planning a large aerial assault in the coming period.

The strike has raised questions about whether NATO could be drawn more directly into the conflict. Romania is a NATO member, meaning an attack on its territory carries significant alliance implications. Diplomatic efforts appear strained as the incident added new tension to an already difficult situation.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Residents of Galati and civilians near the Ukrainian border following a drone strike on a port city

events.title: Civil Unrest in Lima
events.body:
Thousands of people marched through Lima to protest the presidential candidacy of a four-time presidential candidate whose father, a former leader, served 16 years in prison for crimes against humanity. Demonstrators filled the streets in opposition to her bid, reflecting deep divisions over the family's political legacy.

The candidate is described as Peru's most controversial political figure. Her father's conviction and imprisonment have made her candidacy a flashpoint for many Peruvians who remain affected by events from that era.

No violence was reported in the source. The scale of the protest points to significant public opposition heading into the electoral period.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Thousands of protesters in Lima amid a deeply polarizing presidential campaign

events.title: Armed Conflict in Gaza Strip
events.body:
At least 26 Palestinians were killed in the Gaza Strip starting on the eve of Eid, one of the most significant holidays in Islam. The deaths were reported by the UN human rights office on Friday.

The killings occurred over what is typically a time of celebration and family gathering for Muslim communities. The UN human rights office documented the deaths across the holiday period beginning Tuesday.

Civilians in Gaza continue to face danger during what would normally be a time of rest and festivity.
events.source_title: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
events.prayer: At least 26 Palestinians killed in Gaza during the Eid holiday period

events.title: Armed Conflict in Deir al-Balah
events.body:
Two Palestinians were killed and several others wounded in Israeli strikes across Gaza on Saturday, the fourth day of Eid al-Adha. A doctor who led the anaesthesia department at a hospital in Deir al-Balah was killed in a drone strike near a second hospital in the same area. A child was among those injured in the same attack. A second person was killed later in a drone strike near a busy market in Gaza City. Israeli artillery also struck areas near Khan Younis and a refugee camp in central Gaza, while military forces demolished homes in northern Gaza near Beit Lahiya.

In the occupied West Bank, settlers attacked homes in a town south of Nablus, throwing stones and damaging vehicles. Settlers also damaged farmland and trees in the southern West Bank near Hebron. Palestinian officials report at least 922 people have been killed and more than 2,700 injured in Gaza since a ceasefire was announced in October. Settler attacks in the West Bank have also intensified since the war began in October 2023.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Palestinians in Gaza and the West Bank facing ongoing drone strikes, artillery fire, home demolitions, and settler attacks

events.title: Armed Conflict in Nabatieh
events.body:
Israeli forces have pushed north of Lebanon's Litani River and appear to be moving to encircle Nabatieh, a major city in southern Lebanon. An analyst described the Lebanese army as "overly stretched" in its ability to respond to the advancing occupation.

The situation suggests Israeli troops are expanding their foothold in Lebanese territory, placing pressure on both the military and civilian populations in the affected areas. Nabatieh, as a significant population center, could face serious disruption if the reported encirclement moves forward.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Civilians and military forces in Lebanon under pressure as foreign troops advance deeper into southern territory

events.title: Democratic Republic of Congo
events.body:
A serious Ebola outbreak is unfolding in the Democratic Republic of Congo, with the World Health Organization reporting 906 suspected cases and 223 suspected deaths just two weeks into the crisis.

Health officials have noted that Ebola spreads most easily among those caring for the sick, making caregivers — including family members and medical workers — especially vulnerable. The disease's nature means that acts of compassion and love can put people directly at risk.

The scale of the outbreak in just two weeks signals significant pressure on health systems and communities across the affected areas. Hundreds of families are already grieving losses, and responders are working to contain the spread before the numbers climb further.
events.source_title: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
events.prayer: Hundreds of families in the Democratic Republic of Congo grieving Ebola losses as caregivers and health workers face extreme risk

events.title: Armed Conflict in southern Lebanon
events.body:
At least 14 people were killed in Israeli air strikes on towns across southern Lebanon. Four people died in a strike on a building in Abbasiyeh, near the city of Tyre. Eight Syrians were killed in the al-Harthiyeh area near Adloun, one person was killed in Deir Qanoun en-Nahr, and a police officer died in a drone strike on the town of Aba. Additional strikes hit the towns of as-Sarafand, Khirbet Selm, Harayib, Gandouriyeh, Froun, and al-Mansouri.

The attacks came on the same day that Lebanese and Israeli military delegations met at the Pentagon for security talks, and despite a ceasefire arrangement that has been in place since April. The United Nations children's agency reported that 15 children were killed and 62 injured in Lebanon over the past seven days alone, calling the figures "staggering."

Hundreds of thousands of Lebanese have been displaced since March. Several aid organizations warned they may be forced to withdraw from parts of southern Lebanon if conditions continue to worsen, leaving already vulnerable communities without assistance.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Civilians across Lebanon, including children, killed and displaced amid ongoing airstrikes despite a ceasefire arrangement

events.title: Armed Conflict in Abbasiyeh
events.body:
At least five people were killed in Israeli air strikes on towns in southern Lebanon on Friday. Four of those killed died when a building was struck in Abbasiyeh, near the city of Tyre. A fifth person was killed in a separate strike on Deir Qanoun al-Nahr. Additional air raids hit several other southern towns, and Israeli forces also issued evacuation warnings for seven more communities in the region.

The UN children's agency UNICEF reported that 15 children were killed and 62 injured in Lebanon over the prior seven days — an average of 11 children every 24 hours. About 40 hospitals in the south are closed, and medical aid workers say they may be forced to withdraw from some areas due to the security situation. More than 126 civil defence workers have been killed since March.

Hundreds of thousands of Lebanese have been displaced since March. Military delegations from Lebanon and Israel met at the Pentagon the same day, with Lebanon pressing for a halt to the attacks.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Civilians across Lebanon facing deadly airstrikes, displacement, hospital closures, and a rising child death toll

events.title: Armed Conflict in Gaza City
events.body:
Israel's prime minister announced this week that he has directed the military to expand its control of Gaza from roughly 60% to 70% of the territory. He made the statement at a public conference, adding that forces are "pressing from all sides." The move would put Israel in violation of a ceasefire it agreed to in October 2025, under which it withdrew to a set boundary line. Indirect, US-brokered talks between Israel and Hamas aimed at advancing a broader peace plan have stalled.

Strikes have continued inside Gaza despite the ceasefire. Late Wednesday, at least ten people — including five children — were killed in a strike on a building in Gaza City. Israeli officials said the strike targeted senior Hamas figures. A separate strike on Tuesday killed a Hamas military commander along with his wife and two sons. At least 738 Palestinians have been killed since the October ceasefire took effect, according to the Hamas-run health ministry, whose figures the UN considers reliable. The total death toll since the war began in 2023 now stands at more than 72,000, including at least 21,000 children.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Civilians in Gaza, including thousands of children, killed amid ongoing strikes and stalled ceasefire negotiations

events.title: Civil Unrest in Gilgil
events.body:
A fire tore through a dormitory at a girls' boarding school in Gilgil, Kenya, in the early morning hours, killing 16 students and injuring 79 others. Many of the injuries came from students jumping from the upper floor to escape the flames. Seven of the most seriously hurt were transferred to Nairobi for specialized care.

Police arrested eight students identified through witness interviews and CCTV footage as suspected of planning and carrying out the arson. The students were traced to their homes and brought back to the school for questioning. Investigations are ongoing.

The education minister said safety failures made the death toll worse, including overcrowded dormitories and a locked exit door. He dissolved the school's board and ordered action against the headteacher.
